docs.title: Configure Power BI SQL Server Gateway
Purpose
docs.text: Connect Power BI Service to a SQL Server source through the gateway.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Gateway installed and online
  • SQL Server reachable from gateway machine
  • Database credentials available

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Open Power BI Service
  2. Go to Manage Connections and Gateways
  3. Select the gateway
  4. Add a new data source
  5. Choose SQL Server
  6. Enter:
  7. Server name
  8. Database name
  9. Authentication method
  10. Save
  11. Map dataset to the configured data source

docs.title: Important
docs.text: The gateway machine must be able to reach SQL Server on the network.

docs.title: Verification
docs.text: Run a dataset refresh from Power BI Service.

docs.title: Configure an On-Premises Gateway Cluster
docs.text: A gateway cluster groups multiple gateway instances together for high availability and load balancing. If one gateway node goes down, Power BI fails over to another node automatically.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• At least two machines with the on-premises data gateway installed
  • All nodes must be able to reach the same data sources
  • All nodes registered to the same Microsoft 365 / Power BI tenant
  • Gateway version must be the same (or compatible) across all nodes

docs.title: Add a Node to an Existing Gateway Cluster
docs.text:
1. On the second machine, open the On-premises data gateway app after installation.
  2. Sign in with the same account used for the primary gateway.
  3. On the setup screen, select Add to an existing gateway cluster.
  4. From the drop-down list, select the primary gateway cluster to join.
  5. Enter the recovery key that was set when the primary gateway was created.
  6. Click Configure.

Note: The recovery key is set during the initial gateway installation. If you did not save it, you must reset the primary gateway before adding a new node.

docs.title: Verify the Cluster in Power BI Service
docs.text:
1. Open Power BI Service.
  2. Go to Settings → Manage connections and gateways.
  3. Under On-premises data gateways, locate your gateway cluster.
  4. Expand the cluster to confirm all nodes appear with status Online.

docs.title: Configure Load Balancing
docs.text:
By default, Power BI distributes requests across all online nodes randomly. To change the load balancing mode:

  1. In Manage connections and gateways, select the gateway cluster.
  2. Click the three-dot menu (…) → Settings.
  3. Under Load balance requests across all active gateways in this cluster, choose:
  4. Random — requests are distributed equally at random (default)
  5. Performance optimized — Power BI routes requests to the node that responded fastest recently
  6. Click Save.

docs.title: Configure a Data Source on the Cluster
docs.text:
Data sources are shared across all nodes in the cluster. You only need to configure them once:

  1. In Manage connections and gateways, select the gateway cluster.
  2. Click New connection.
  3. Choose SQL Server as the connection type.
  4. Enter:
  5. Server name — hostname or IP of the SQL Server
  6. Database name
  7. Authentication — Windows or Basic
  8. Click Create.

All nodes in the cluster will use this connection definition automatically.

docs.title: Maintenance: Take a Node Offline
docs.text:
To update or restart a single node without interrupting service:

  1. In Manage connections and gateways, expand the cluster.
  2. Select the specific node and click Disable.
  3. Perform maintenance on that machine.
  4. Restart the gateway service on the machine: open the gateway app and confirm it shows Online.
  5. Re-enable the node in Power BI Service.

docs.title: Troubleshooting Cluster Nodes
docs.text: Symptom Likely Cause Action Node shows Offline Gateway service stopped Restart the on-premises data gateway service on that machine Node not visible after adding Wrong recovery key or account Delete the gateway entry and re-add with correct credentials All refreshes fail All cluster nodes offline Check network connectivity and gateway service on each node Node added but not joining cluster Version mismatch Update gateway to the same version on all nodes

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Install the on-premises data gateway to connect Power BI Service to local data sources.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Windows machine that is always on
  • Power BI account
  • Internet access
  • Access to local SQL Server

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Download On-premises data gateway
  2. Run the installer
  3. Sign in with your Power BI account
  4. Register the gateway
  5. Give the gateway a clear name
  6. Set a recovery key

docs.title: Recommended Host
docs.text: Install the gateway on: - A stable server - A machine with reliable connectivity to SQL Server - A machine not frequently powered off

docs.title: Next Step
docs.text: Configure the SQL Server data source in Power BI Service.

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
• Gateway offline
  • Invalid credentials
  • SQL Server unreachable
  • Port 1433 blocked
  • Dataset not mapped to gateway source

docs.title: Checks
docs.text:
1. Confirm gateway status is online
  2. Confirm SQL Server is reachable from gateway host
  3. Confirm credentials are valid
  4. Confirm server and database names match exactly
  5. Confirm dataset is mapped to the correct data source

docs.title: Network Test
docs.text:
From the gateway machine:

```powershell Test-NetConnection -ComputerName -Port 1433

docs.title: BigQuery to SQL Server Connectivity Checklist
Purpose
docs.text: Validate all prerequisites for a data sync process writing into SQL Server.

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
• SQL Server remote connections enabled
  • TCP/IP enabled
  • SQL Server static port configured
  • Firewall inbound rule open for 1433
  • External/public access configured if required
  • Source system IP whitelisted
  • SQL login available
  • Database permissions granted
  • Connectivity tested successfully

docs.title: Example External Source
docs.text:
• Source IP: 34.89.7.83
  • Destination Port: 1433

docs.title: Validation Command
docs.text: ```powershell Test-NetConnection -ComputerName -Port 1433

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Allow a known external IP to access a service securely.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Whitelist:

  • IP: 34.89.7.83
  • Port: 1433
  • Protocol: TCP

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Open firewall or perimeter device configuration
  2. Add inbound rule
  3. Restrict source to the approved public IP
  4. Restrict destination to required internal server
  5. Restrict destination port to required port
  6. Save and apply

docs.title: Verification
docs.text: Test from approved source IP only.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Never open SQL Server broadly to the internet
  • Prefer source-IP-restricted rules
  • Review whitelist entries regularly

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Expose a service hosted on a private VM or server behind NAT.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: Forward public port 1433 to internal server 10.168.1.40:1433

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Log in to the router or firewall
  2. Open Port Forwarding or NAT Rules
  3. Create a new rule:
  4. External Port: 1433
  5. Internal IP: 10.168.1.40
  6. Internal Port: 1433
  7. Protocol: TCP
  8. Save and apply configuration

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
From outside the local network:

```powershell Test-NetConnection -ComputerName -Port 1433

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Check whether a host and port are reachable.

docs.title: PowerShell
docs.text: ```powershell Test-NetConnection -ComputerName -Port

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Restrict service access to trusted source IP addresses.

docs.title: Typical Use Cases
docs.text:
• External data sync service
  • Power BI gateway dependencies
  • Vendor-managed integrations

docs.title: Example Rule
docs.text: Allow inbound TCP 1433 from 34.89.7.83 only.

docs.title: Benefits
docs.text:
• Reduces attack surface
  • Limits exposure
  • Supports controlled partner connectivity

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Keep whitelist entries documented
  • Remove unused IPs
  • Pair with strong credentials and encryption

docs.title: Allow Remote Connections to SQL Server
Purpose
docs.text: Allow SQL Server to accept connections from other machines on the network or internet.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Open SQL Server Management Studio
  2. Connect to the SQL Server instance
  3. Right-click the server
  4. Select Properties
  5. Open the Connections page
  6. Ensure Allow remote connections to this server is enabled

docs.title: SQL Authentication
docs.text: If SQL logins are required: 1. Right-click server 2. Select Properties 3. Open Security 4. Choose SQL Server and Windows Authentication mode 5. Restart SQL Server

docs.title: Verification
docs.text: Try connecting from another machine using: - Server IP - SQL login - Port 1433

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Remote connections also depend on: - TCP/IP enabled - Firewall open - Correct router/NAT configuration

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Set SQL Server to listen on a fixed TCP port, usually 1433.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Open SQL Server Configuration Manager
  2. Go to SQL Server Network Configuration
  3. Open Protocols for MSSQLSERVER
  4. Double-click TCP/IP
  5. Open the IP Addresses tab
  6. Scroll to IPAll
  7. Clear TCP Dynamic Ports
  8. Set TCP Port to 1433

docs.title: Restart
docs.text: Restart the SQL Server service after saving changes.

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
Check connectivity:

```powershell Test-NetConnection -ComputerName -Port 1433

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Enable TCP/IP networking so SQL Server can accept remote connections.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• SQL Server installed
  • Access to SQL Server Configuration Manager
  • Local administrator permissions

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Open SQL Server Configuration Manager
  2. Go to SQL Server Network Configuration
  3. Select Protocols for MSSQLSERVER
  4. Right-click TCP/IP
  5. Click Enable

docs.title: Restart SQL Server
docs.text:
1. Open SQL Server Services
  2. Restart SQL Server (MSSQLSERVER)

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
Run:

```powershell Test-NetConnection -ComputerName -Port 1433

docs.title: Open Windows Firewall for SQL Server
Purpose
docs.text: Allow inbound SQL Server traffic through Windows Firewall.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Open Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security
  2. Select Inbound Rules
  3. Click New Rule
  4. Choose Port
  5. Select TCP
  6. Enter 1433
  7. Choose Allow the connection
  8. Apply to required profiles
  9. Name the rule SQL Server TCP 1433

docs.title: Optional
docs.text: Add outbound rule if required by policy.

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
From another machine:

```powershell Test-NetConnection -ComputerName -Port 1433

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Publish a SQL Server instance running inside a VM for remote access.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Enable TCP/IP in SQL Server
  2. Set static port 1433
  3. Restart SQL Server
  4. Open Windows Firewall inbound TCP 1433
  5. Configure router/firewall NAT forwarding to the VM
  6. Restrict external access by source IP where possible

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
From an external network:

```powershell Test-NetConnection -ComputerName -Port 1433

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Understand how to access services hosted on a VM behind a router or NAT device.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: A VM may have a private IP such as 10.168.1.40 which is not reachable directly from the internet.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Public IP on the router or firewall
  • Port forwarding configured
  • Firewall rule allowing the traffic
  • Service listening on the expected port

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
• SQL Server TCP/IP enabled
  • SQL Server listening on port 1433
  • Windows Firewall allows 1433
  • Router forwards 1433 to VM private IP
  • External firewall or ISP does not block 1433

docs.title: Verification
docs.text: Test using the public IP, not the private IP.

docs.title: Setup ngrok HTTP Tunnel
docs.text: ngrok is a command-line application that exposes local ports to the internet via secure tunnels. This guide covers installation, configuration, and best practices.

docs.title: Option 1: Chocolatey
docs.text: choco install ngrok

docs.title: Option 2: Direct Download
docs.text:
1. Download from ngrok.com
  2. Extract the executable
  3. Add to PATH or run directly

docs.title: Option 3: Scoop
docs.text: scoop install ngrok

docs.title: Mac
docs.text: brew install ngrok

docs.title: Linux
docs.text: curl -s https://ngrok-agent.s3.amazonaws.com/ngrok.asc | sudo tee /etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d/ngrok.asc >/dev/null && echo "deb https://ngrok-agent.s3.amazonaws.com buster main" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/ngrok.list && sudo apt update && sudo apt install ngrok

docs.title: Get Your Auth Token
docs.text:
1. Sign up at ngrok.com
  2. Go to Dashboard
  3. Copy your authtoken

docs.title: Configure ngrok
docs.text:
ngrok config add-authtoken <your-authtoken>

This stores the token in ~/.ngrok2/ngrok.yml for future use.

docs.title: Start HTTP Tunnel
docs.text:
ngrok http 8080

Output:

ngrok                                                                 (Ctrl+C to quit)

Session Status                online
Account                       user@example.com
Version                       3.x.x
Region                        us (United States)
Forwarding                    https://1234-5678-abcd.ngrok.io -> http://localhost:8080
Forwarding                    http://1234-5678-abcd.ngrok.io -> http://localhost:8080

docs.title: Host Header Rewrite
docs.text: The --host-header=rewrite flag is critical for most APIs.

docs.title: Problem Without It
docs.text: When a request comes through the tunnel: - External URL: https://1234-5678-abcd.ngrok.io - Request header shows Host: 1234-5678-abcd.ngrok.io - Your API expects Host: localhost or a specific domain - Header validation fails → API rejects the request

docs.title: Solution With It
docs.text:
ngrok http 8080 --host-header=rewrite

How it works: - External request arrives with public Host header - ngrok intercepts and rewrites to Host: localhost:8080 - Your API receives the expected header - Request succeeds ✓

docs.title: When You Need It
docs.text:
• ASP.NET Core APIs with host validation
  • APIs checking origin headers (CORS, WebSocket)
  • Session cookies tied to specific hosts
  • APIs with security policies validating Host headers

docs.title: When It's Optional
docs.text:
• REST APIs without host validation
  • Stateless microservices
  • APIs accepting any origin

Recommendation: Always use --host-header=rewrite unless you have a specific reason not to.

docs.title: Custom Domain
docs.text:
Use a paid ngrok account to reserve custom domains:

ngrok http 8080 --domain=myapi.ngrok.io

docs.title: Custom Subdomain
docs.text:
ngrok http 8080 --subdomain=myapi

Results in: https://myapi.ngrok.io

docs.title: Basic Authentication
docs.text:
Add password protection:

ngrok http 8080 --basic-auth=user:password

Testers must provide credentials:

curl -u user:password https://myapi.ngrok.io/api/data

docs.title: Custom Headers
docs.text:
Add headers to tunneled requests:

ngrok http 8080 --request-header-add "X-Custom-Header: value"

docs.title: Region Selection
docs.text:
Choose closer region for lower latency:

# List available regions
ngrok http --help | grep region

# Use specific region
ngrok http 8080 --region=eu

Available regions: us, eu, au, ap, jp, in, sa

docs.title: Multiple Tunnels
docs.text:
Start ngrok with config file for multiple tunnels:

~/.ngrok2/ngrok.yml:

authtoken: your-token
tunnels:
  api:
    proto: http
    addr: 8080
    host_header: rewrite
  web:
    proto: http
    addr: 3000
    host_header: rewrite

Run all:

ngrok start --all

Run specific:

ngrok start api

docs.title: Web Inspection Interface
docs.text:
ngrok provides a local web dashboard at http://127.0.0.1:4040

View: - All HTTP requests/responses - Request headers and body - Response code and latency - Real-time traffic monitoring

docs.title: Command Line Inspection
docs.text:
# View all tunnels
ngrok tunnels list

# View session details
ngrok config list

docs.title: Logs
docs.text:
# View logs
ngrok config list

# Log file location
# Windows: %AppData%/ngrok/ngrok.log
# Mac/Linux: ~/.ngrok2/ngrok.log

docs.title: ASP.NET Core API
docs.text:
# Terminal 1: Start API
dotnet run --urls=http://localhost:5000

# Terminal 2: Start tunnel
ngrok http 5000 --host-header=rewrite

# Share: https://1234-5678-abcd.ngrok.io

docs.title: Node.js Express API
docs.text:
# Terminal 1: Start API
npm start  # Runs on port 3000

# Terminal 2: Start tunnel
ngrok http 3000 --host-header=rewrite

# Share: https://1234-5678-abcd.ngrok.io

docs.title: Python Flask API
docs.text:
# Terminal 1: Start API
python app.py  # Runs on port 5000

# Terminal 2: Start tunnel
ngrok http 5000 --host-header=rewrite --basic-auth=admin:password

# Share with authentication: https://admin:password@1234-5678-abcd.ngrok.io

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text: Practice Why How Always use --host-header=rewrite Prevents host validation errors Add to every command Protect sensitive APIs Prevent unauthorized access Use --basic-auth or IP whitelist Monitor traffic Detect issues early Check http://localhost:4040 Use HTTPS only Encrypt data in transit ngrok provides HTTPS by default Rotate tokens Prevent account compromise Change monthly Stop when done Prevent unintended access Ctrl+C to stop tunnel Test before sharing Verify API works Test locally first, then through tunnel

docs.title: Connection Refused
docs.text:
# Verify API is running
Get-Process | Where-Object {$_.Name -like "*dotnet*"}

# Check port is listening
netstat -ano | Select-String 5000

docs.title: Tunnel Won't Start
docs.text:
# Check authentication
ngrok config list

# Verify auth token
ngrok config add-authtoken <your-new-token>

docs.title: External Access Fails
docs.text:
1. Verify tunnel started successfully
  2. Check ngrok dashboard: http://localhost:4040
  3. Test with curl: powershell curl -v https://your-tunnel-url/api/test
  4. Check API logs for errors

docs.title: Slow Response Times
docs.text:
• Check network latency
  • Try different region: ngrok http 8080 --region=eu
  • Monitor via dashboard

docs.title: Related Documentation
docs.text:
• Publish Local API for Testing
  • Setup Dev Tunnels

docs.title: SQL Server Connection Timeout
docs.text: Connection timeouts when accessing SQL Server can occur due to network, firewall, or server configuration issues. This guide helps diagnose and resolve these problems.

docs.title: Common Causes
docs.text:
• Firewall blocking connections - Windows Firewall or network firewall blocking traffic
  • SQL Server not listening - TCP/IP protocol not enabled
  • Incorrect connection string - Wrong server name, port, or credentials
  • Port not accessible - SQL Server listening on non-standard port that isn't open
  • Network connectivity issues - Network path unreachable or routing problems
  • SQL Server service stopped - Service not running

docs.title: 1. Verify SQL Server is Running
docs.text:
# Check SQL Server service status
Get-Service -Name MSSQLSERVER

The status should show "Running". If not, start the service:

Start-Service -Name MSSQLSERVER

docs.title: 2. Check TCP/IP is Enabled
docs.text: TCP/IP must be enabled for remote connections. See Enable TCP/IP.

docs.title: 3. Test Network Connectivity
docs.text:
Test if the port is reachable:

Test-NetConnection -ComputerName <server-name> -Port 1433

If TcpTestSucceeded is False, see TCP Test Succeeded False.

docs.title: 4. Verify Firewall Rules
docs.text: Check that Windows Firewall allows SQL Server traffic. See Open Windows Firewall for SQL Server.

docs.title: 5. Check Network Firewall
docs.text:
If the server is behind a network firewall, ensure: - Port 1433 (default) is open - Source IP is whitelisted

See Firewall Whitelisting and IP Whitelisting.

docs.title: For Local/LAN Connections
docs.text:
1. Verify SQL Server service is running
  2. Enable TCP/IP protocol
  3. Configure static port if using named instance
  4. Open Windows Firewall for SQL Server

docs.title: For Remote/Internet Connections
docs.text:
1. Complete all local steps above
  2. Configure network firewall rules
  3. Use port forwarding or tunneling if behind NAT
  4. Consider using Dev Tunnels or ngrok for testing

See: - Expose SQL Server from VM - VM Behind NAT Access - Setup Dev Tunnels - Setup ngrok HTTP Tunnel

docs.title: For Power BI Connections
docs.text:
If using Power BI: 1. Install Power BI Gateway on network with SQL Server access 2. Configure gateway with SQL Server credentials 3. Ensure gateway machine has network access to SQL Server

See Configure Power BI SQL Server Gateway.

docs.title: Related Documentation
docs.text:
• Enable TCP/IP
  • Allow Remote Connections
  • Configure Static Port 1433
  • Open Windows Firewall for SQL Server
  • Test Port Connectivity
  • TCP Test Succeeded False

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
Test-NetConnection returns:

TcpTestSucceeded : False

docs.title: Use Case: Allow External Data Sync to SQL Server
Scenario
docs.text:
A third-party data pipeline needs to write data into a SQL Server database hosted inside a private network.

Example:

BigQuery ETL job → SQL Server

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• External system IP must be whitelisted
  • SQL Server must allow remote connections
  • TCP/IP protocol enabled
  • Port 1433 reachable

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Enable TCP/IP in SQL Server
  2. Configure static port 1433
  3. Open Windows Firewall inbound rule
  4. Configure router port forwarding
  5. Whitelist external IP address

Example:

Source IP: 34.89.7.83

docs.title: Connectivity Test
docs.text:
Run:

```powershell Test-NetConnection -ComputerName -Port 1433

docs.title: Use Case: Connect Power BI to On-Premises SQL Server
Scenario
docs.text:
An organization wants to build Power BI dashboards using a SQL Server database hosted on a local server or virtual machine.

Because the database is not publicly accessible, Power BI Service cannot connect directly.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text: Use the Power BI On-Premises Data Gateway.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Install Power BI Gateway on a machine with network access to SQL Server.
  2. Enable TCP/IP on SQL Server.
  3. Allow remote connections in SQL Server.
  4. Ensure port 1433 is open between the gateway and SQL Server.
  5. Configure a SQL Server data source in Power BI Service.
  6. Map the dataset to the gateway data source.

docs.title: Related Runbooks
docs.text:
• install-powerbi-gateway.md
  • configure-powerbi-sql-server-gateway.md
  • enable-tcp-ip.md
  • open-windows-firewall-for-sql-server.md

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
1. Trigger a manual dataset refresh in Power BI Service.
  2. Confirm the refresh completes successfully.

docs.title: Common Problems
docs.text: Problem Cause Gateway offline Gateway service not running Login failed Wrong SQL credentials Timeout Firewall blocking port 1433

docs.title: Use Case: Publish Local API for External Testing
Scenario
docs.text:
A developer needs to expose a locally running API so external systems can test integration with real-time changes during development.

Example:

Local ASP.NET API running on http://localhost:5000 needs to be accessible to external testers or third-party integrations.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Use a tunneling tool to create a secure, publicly accessible URL:

  • ngrok - Simple, reliable tunneling with custom domains
  • Dev Tunnels - Microsoft's built-in tunneling for Visual Studio

docs.title: 1. Install ngrok
docs.text:
Download from ngrok.com or use package manager:

# Using Chocolatey on Windows
choco install ngrok

# Or download binary and add to PATH

docs.title: 2. Authenticate
docs.text:
Get your authtoken from ngrok dashboard.

ngrok config add-authtoken <your-authtoken>

docs.title: 3. Start Your Local API
docs.text:
Ensure your API is running:

# Example: ASP.NET Core API
dotnet run --urls=http://localhost:5000

docs.title: 4. Start Tunnel
docs.text:
ngrok http 5000 --host-header=rewrite

Output will show:

Session Status          online
Account                 your-email@example.com
Version                 3.x.x
Region                  us (United States)
Forwarding              https://abcd-1234-5678.ngrok.io -> http://localhost:5000

Your API is now accessible at: https://abcd-1234-5678.ngrok.io

For detailed information about the --host-header=rewrite flag and other ngrok options, see Setup ngrok HTTP Tunnel.

docs.title: 5. Share Public URL
docs.text: Share the forwarding URL with external testers. They can access your local API without additional setup.

docs.title: Steps (Dev Tunnels)
docs.text: Dev Tunnels is Microsoft's native tunneling solution, integrated with Visual Studio.

docs.title: 1. Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Visual Studio 2022 (Update 7 or later)
  • Signed in to Microsoft Account

docs.title: 2. Create a Dev Tunnel
docs.text:
In Visual Studio:

  1. Click the Run dropdown → Create Tunnel...
  2. Select tunnel type: Temporary or Persistent
  3. Enter a name for your tunnel

docs.title: 3. Start Your API
docs.text:
Run your application normally in Visual Studio:

# If using command line
dotnet run --urls=http://localhost:5000

docs.title: 4. Access the Tunnel URL
docs.text:
Visual Studio displays a tunnel URL:

https://your-tunnel-name-abcdef.devtunnels.ms

Alternative method using CLI:

devtunnel create --name my-api
devtunnel port create -p 5000
devtunnel host

docs.title: 5. Share with Testers
docs.text: Share the tunnel URL. External systems can connect without knowing your local IP or machine.

docs.title: Verify Tunnel is Active
docs.text:
# Test endpoint from local machine
Invoke-WebRequest -Uri "http://localhost:5000/api/health" -Verbose

# Test endpoint from public URL
Invoke-WebRequest -Uri "https://your-tunnel-url/api/health" -Verbose

docs.title: Monitor API Traffic
docs.text:
ngrok: Web dashboard at http://localhost:4040

Requests | Response Code | Method | Path | Duration

Dev Tunnels: Monitor in Visual Studio Output window

docs.title: Test with External Tools
docs.text:
Use Postman, cURL, or your test runner to hit the public URL:

curl -X GET https://your-tunnel-url/api/health
curl -X POST https://your-tunnel-url/api/users \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"name":"John","email":"john@example.com"}'

docs.title: Verify Headers & Auth
docs.text:
Ensure proper headers are passed through the tunnel:

$headers = @{
    "Authorization" = "Bearer token123"
    "Content-Type" = "application/json"
}

Invoke-WebRequest `
  -Uri "https://your-tunnel-url/api/data" `
  -Headers $headers `
  -Method GET

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text: Practice Why How Use HTTPS only Tunnels use HTTPS to encrypt traffic Verify URL starts with https:// Rotate auth tokens Prevent unauthorized access Change ngrok/Dev Tunnel tokens monthly Monitor activity Detect suspicious requests Check ngrok dashboard or logs Use temporary URLs Limit exposure duration Use ngrok temp tunnels or Dev Tunnels sessions Disable when done Stop unintended access Stop tunnel process after testing

docs.title: Connection Refused
docs.text:
Ensure your local API is running and listening on the correct port:

# Check if port is listening
netstat -an | Select-String 5000

docs.title: Tunnel Won't Start
docs.text:
# Check for port conflicts
Get-Process | Where-Object {$_.Id -eq (Get-NetTCPConnection -LocalPort 5000).OwningProcess}

# Restart ngrok or dev tunnel
# Kill previous process and start fresh

docs.title: External Access Fails
docs.text:
• Verify firewall isn't blocking localhost
  • Confirm tunnel URL is correct
  • Check API logs for request details
  • Test with curl from another machine
